hypothesis: Using multi-modal data (both image and text features) will improve the accuracy of model predictions for patient disease diagnosis compared to models using only text-based electronic health records (EHR) data.
domain: Healthcare / Clinical ML for disease diagnosis
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal vs. Text-Only Disease Diagnosis

### 1. Data Acquisition and Labeling

We would source patient records from the hospital system spanning 24 months of patient episodes. The dataset includes structured EHR fields (demographics, laboratory values, vital signs), unstructured clinical notes, and linked radiological images (DICOM format). All records are aligned by unique patient identifier and timestamped at the point of imaging or clinical order.

Disease labels are derived from confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) established post-hoc at the point of clinical confirmation (e.g., pathology result, radiologist consensus reading, or documented clinical diagnosis). Cases lacking confirmatory evidence or with conflicting labels are excluded. This ensures ground-truth labels reflect actual clinical outcomes, not provisional assessments.

### 2. Stratified Random Split Strategy

We divide the 24-month dataset by selecting a stratified random sample across all months: 70% of records for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing. This stratified random sampling ensures consistent class distribution mitigating imbalance, reflecting real-world deployment. This approach supports robust cross-sectional analysis while controlling for temporal class variance.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured EHR fields (age, sex, lab values) are standardized using z-score normalization. Clinical notes are vectorized using TF-IDF with bigrams (1–2 grams) and English stop-word removal. All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting to ensure stable parameter estimates. This results in consistent transformations applied uniformly during model training and evaluation.

Radiological images are normalized using whole-set DICOM statistics (Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI; intensity scaling for X-ray). Images are resized to 256×256 pixels. Features are extracted via a ResNet-50 convolutional base (pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned during training) yielding 2048-dimensional embeddings per image. Structured fields, text TF-IDF vectors, and image embeddings are concatenated into a unified feature matrix. No features encode the label or post-diagnosis information.

### 4. Baseline and Model Selection

The baseline model is text-only: logistic regression or XGBoost trained on structured EHR fields and clinical note TF-IDF vectors. The baseline receives identical preprocessing, class weighting, and hyperparameter tuning effort as the multi-modal model. Both models are tuned via Bayesian optimization over equivalent budgets (e.g., 50 iterations) on the validation set, selecting hyperparameters that maximize macro-F1.

The multi-modal model is a neural network with three branches: (1) dense layers on structured fields, (2) learned text embeddings via attention over note tokens, (3) CNN fine-tuning on images. Branches merge via concatenation and pass through 2–3 dense layers (dropout 0.3–0.5) to multi-class softmax output. Both models use class-weighted cross-entropy loss to handle imbalance.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score. In multi-class diagnosis with imbalance and high stakes (missing rare-disease diagnoses is costly), macro-F1 ensures model performance on minority classes is not masked by accuracy on common diseases. It reflects the stakeholder objective: improving diagnostic accuracy for all disease classes, especially underrepresented ones.

Secondary metrics include weighted F1, per-class precision/recall, macro-AUROC, balanced accuracy, and calibration (Brier score). Per-class metrics enable diagnosis-specific improvement assessment.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

During development, hyperparameters are selected on the validation set (15% split) using macro-F1. Early stopping is applied during neural network training if validation loss stalls for 10 epochs. The test set is sealed and untouched until final evaluation.

After both models are finalized, predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Metrics are computed on these held-out predictions. Results are reported with 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. No threshold adjustments or post-hoc tuning on the test set is permitted.

### 7. Confound Control

Clinician diagnostic practice variation is controlled by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by clinician/department to ensure representation; (2) including clinician/department as a categorical feature; (3) performing sensitivity analysis to detect department-specific effects. Imaging protocol variation is minimized by normalizing using full data statistics and including modality as a feature. Population drift is assessed via distribution shift tests on feature distributions between training and test periods.

### 8. Scope

This design establishes whether multi-modal data *improves diagnostic accuracy* (macro-F1) beyond text-only EHR data, controlling for model complexity and tuning effort. It does not establish causality or generalizability beyond the hospital system or time period studied.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses a random 70/15/15 sample across all months rather than a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage: the model may be trained on data from later time points and evaluated on earlier ones, meaning it can learn from future patient episodes that would be inaccessible in real deployment. For longitudinal healthcare data where disease patterns and clinical practices evolve, this artificially inflates performance and undermines real-world generalization.

All preprocessing steps—including normalization parameters, text vectorizer vocabulary, and image statistics—are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. This means the test set influences the scaling, encoding, and feature extraction processes, leading to information leakage. As a result, the model's evaluation is overly optimistic and does not reflect true performance on unseen data, especially in a scenario with temporal evolution or population drift.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratification by disease class in the corrupted design introduces artificial balance and risks over-representing rare diseases, compromising realism.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by class is methodologically sound in imbalanced diagnostic settings and is explicitly called for by the design challenges in the hypothesis. The real issue is not stratification itself, but the lack of temporal ordering—this corrupted version incorrectly uses random sampling across time, which invalidates the evaluation timeline.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it overweights rare diseases and may not reflect overall clinical utility.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is appropriate here due to the high cost of missing rare but serious diseases, as stated in the stakeholder objective and hypothesis context. A critic arguing against macro-F1 without acknowledging clinical stakes would be misaligned with domain priorities and should not be credited.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the model evaluation risks temporal leakage due to non-chronological data splitting, and that preprocessing leakage further compromises test set integrity. The final verdict concludes that both flaws invalidate the reliability of the reported performance gain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that training on future data and evaluating on past data violates real-world deployment assumptions and causes overfitting to future trends; and that preprocessing on full data allows the test set to influence training transformations.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A full and correct evaluation would require a temporal + stratified split (chronological train/val/test) and preprocessing fit only on the training set. The reviewer demands these changes to ensure valid generalization assessment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer proposes correct fixes: (1) use months 1–18 for training, 19–21 for validation, 22–24 for test, with stratification by disease within each; (2) recompute all preprocessing parameters on training data alone, then apply to val/test sets.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal vs. Text-Only Disease Diagnosis

### 1. Data Acquisition and Labeling

We would source patient records from the hospital system spanning 24 months of patient episodes. The dataset includes structured EHR fields (demographics, laboratory values, vital signs), unstructured clinical notes, and linked radiological images (DICOM format). All records are aligned by unique patient identifier and timestamped at the point of imaging or clinical order.

Disease labels are derived from confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) established post-hoc at the point of clinical confirmation (e.g., pathology result, radiologist consensus reading, or documented clinical diagnosis). Cases lacking confirmatory evidence or with conflicting labels are excluded. This ensures ground-truth labels reflect actual clinical outcomes, not provisional assessments.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

We divide the 24-month dataset chronologically: months 1–18 form the training set, months 19–21 the validation set, and months 22–24 the test set. This temporal split prevents temporal leakage—the model cannot learn from future information—and reflects real-world deployment where the model must generalize to subsequent patient cohorts.

Within each temporal window, we apply stratification by disease class to ensure each split maintains proportional representation. This addresses class imbalance: if a rare disease comprises 5% of the population, it comprises approximately 5% of each split, preventing the model from learning a trivial bias toward common classes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured EHR fields (age, sex, lab values) are standardized using z-score normalization. Clinical notes are vectorized using TF-IDF with bigrams (1–2 grams) and English stop-word removal. All preprocessing parameters (scaling statistics, vocabulary) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without modification.

Radiological images are normalized using training-set DICOM statistics (Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI; intensity scaling for X-ray). Images are resized to 256×256 pixels. Features are extracted via a ResNet-50 convolutional base (pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned during training) yielding 2048-dimensional embeddings per image. Structured fields, text TF-IDF vectors, and image embeddings are concatenated into a unified feature matrix. No features encode the label or post-diagnosis information.

### 4. Baseline and Model Selection

The baseline model is text-only: logistic regression or XGBoost trained on structured EHR fields and clinical note TF-IDF vectors. The baseline receives identical preprocessing, class weighting, and hyperparameter tuning effort as the multi-modal model. Both models are tuned via Bayesian optimization over equivalent budgets (e.g., 50 iterations) on the validation set, selecting hyperparameters that maximize macro-F1.

The multi-modal model is a neural network with three branches: (1) dense layers on structured fields, (2) learned text embeddings via attention over note tokens, (3) CNN fine-tuning on images. Branches merge via concatenation and pass through 2–3 dense layers (dropout 0.3–0.5) to multi-class softmax output. Both models use class-weighted cross-entropy loss to handle imbalance.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score. In multi-class diagnosis with imbalance and high stakes (missing rare-disease diagnoses is costly), macro-F1 ensures model performance on minority classes is not masked by accuracy on common diseases. It reflects the stakeholder objective: improving diagnostic accuracy for all disease classes, especially underrepresented ones.

Secondary metrics include weighted F1, per-class precision/recall, macro-AUROC, balanced accuracy, and calibration (Brier score). Per-class metrics enable diagnosis-specific improvement assessment.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

During development, hyperparameters are selected on the validation set (months 19–21) using macro-F1. Early stopping is applied during neural network training if validation loss stalls for 10 epochs. The test set is sealed and untouched until final evaluation.

After both models are finalized, predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Metrics are computed on these held-out predictions. Results are reported with 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. No threshold adjustments or post-hoc tuning on the test set is permitted.

### 7. Confound Control

Clinician diagnostic practice variation is controlled by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by clinician/department to ensure representation; (2) including clinician/department as a categorical feature; (3) performing sensitivity analysis to detect department-specific effects. Imaging protocol variation is minimized by normalizing using training statistics and including modality as a feature. Population drift is assessed via distribution shift tests on feature distributions between training and test periods.

### 8. Scope

This design establishes whether multi-modal data *improves diagnostic accuracy* (macro-F1) beyond text-only EHR data, controlling for model complexity and tuning effort. It does not establish causality or generalizability beyond the hospital system or time period studied.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtly difficult because both corruptions appear benign and are often used in non-temporal domains. A reviewer must distinguish between sound preprocessing goals (stable estimates) and methodological integrity, and recognize that 'robust cross-sectional analysis' is inappropriate for longitudinal clinical data where time order matters.
